instrument.model: DC magnetization vs T (zero-field-cooled), inductive susceptometer (literature extraction)

sample_provenance.batch_lot: Finnemore/Stromberg/Swenson high-purity Nb bulk (Ames Lab, ~1965 vintage); also reproduced on standard Nb across NIST cryogenic materials database.

provenance.source_citation: D. K. Finnemore, T. F. Stromberg, C. A. Swenson, 'Superconducting Properties of High-Purity Niobium', Physical Review 149, 231 (1966). Tc=9.25 K and full Meissner state below 8 K widely reproduced; see also Tinkham, 'Introduction to Superconductivity', 2nd ed., Table 1.1 (McGraw-Hill, 1996, ISBN 978-0486435039).

scope_caveats: Digitized from published magnetization figure family (Finnemore et al. 1966 + Tinkham textbook Ch. 1 reproduction); chi_volumetric values ±5%.

chi_volumetric is the dimensionless SI volume susceptibility from zero-field-cooled (ZFC) Meissner-state shielding. The chi=-1 limit at T<<Tc assumes full flux expulsion (bulk Nb, demag-corrected). Sample with finite demag or pinning will read |chi| < 1.
Tc value 9.25 K is the canonical Nb transition midpoint (high-purity bulk); thin films / strained samples report 9.0-9.3 K.
replication_count reflects 60+ years of independent reproduction across cryogenic-physics labs (NIST cryogenic-materials database lists Nb as a primary fixed point).
Single bulk-sample literature value; not lot-tested per-batch (vendor Nb purity matters: RRR > 100 typical for the cited value).
citations: Finnemore, Stromberg, Swenson, Phys. Rev. 149, 231 (1966), https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRev.149.231
M. Tinkham, 'Introduction to Superconductivity', 2nd ed., McGraw-Hill (1996), ISBN 978-0486435039 — Table 1.1 Nb properties + Ch. 1 Meissner discussion
NIST Cryogenic Materials Properties database, https://trc.nist.gov/cryogenics/materials/materialproperties.htm — Nb listed as reference cryogenic material
